From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

British revolution of 1688

This article is about the English revolution of 1688. For the corresponding events in Scotland, see [Glorious Revolution in Scotland](/wiki/Glorious_Revolution_in_Scotland "Glorious Revolution in Scotland"). For the revolution of 1868 in Spain, see [Glorious Revolution (Spain)](/wiki/Glorious_Revolution_(Spain) "Glorious Revolution (Spain)"). For other uses, see [Glorious Revolution (disambiguation)](/wiki/Glorious_Revolution_(disambiguation) "Glorious Revolution (disambiguation)").

"The Bloodless Revolution" redirects here. For the book on vegetarian history, see [The Bloodless Revolution (book)](/wiki/The_Bloodless_Revolution_(book) "The Bloodless Revolution (book)").

The **Glorious Revolution**[[a]](#cite_note-1) was the [deposition](/wiki/Deposition_(politics) "Deposition (politics)") of King [James II](/wiki/James_II_of_England "James II of England") in November 1688. He was replaced by his daughter [Mary II](/wiki/Mary_II "Mary II") and her Dutch husband, James's nephew [William III of Orange](/wiki/William_III_of_Orange "William III of Orange"). The two ruled as [joint monarchs](/wiki/Joint_monarchs "Joint monarchs") of [England](/wiki/Kingdom_of_England "Kingdom of England"), [Scotland](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Scotland "Kingdom of Scotland"), and [Ireland](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Ireland "Kingdom of Ireland") until Mary's death in 1694, when William became ruler in his own right. [Jacobitism](/wiki/Jacobitism "Jacobitism"), the political movement that aimed to restore the exiled James or his descendants of the [House of Stuart](/wiki/House_of_Stuart "House of Stuart") to the throne, persisted into the late 18th century. Some historians consider it the last successful [invasion of England](/wiki/Invasion_of_England "Invasion of England").[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDillon2007213-2)

Despite being a [Catholic](/wiki/Catholicism "Catholicism"), James became king in February 1685 with widespread backing from the [Protestant](/wiki/Protestant "Protestant") majorities in England and Scotland, as well as largely Catholic Ireland, but his policies quickly eroded support. The prospect of a Catholic [dynasty](/wiki/Dynasty "Dynasty") following the birth of his son [James Francis Edward Stuart](/wiki/James_Francis_Edward_Stuart "James Francis Edward Stuart") on 10 June 1688 led some of his domestic opponents to issue the [Invitation to William](/wiki/Invitation_to_William "Invitation to William"), seeking Dutch support to remove him.

The [Dutch States General](/wiki/Dutch_States_General "Dutch States General") and William were concerned that James might support [France](/wiki/Kingdom_of_France "Kingdom of France") in the [Nine Years' War](/wiki/Nine_Years%27_War "Nine Years' War"). Exploiting unrest in England and claiming to be responding to the invitation, William landed in [Devon](/wiki/Devon "Devon") on 5 November 1688. As William advanced on London, James's army disintegrated and he went into exile in [France](/wiki/Kingdom_of_France "Kingdom of France") on 23 December. In April 1689, [Parliament](/wiki/Convention_Parliament_(1689) "Convention Parliament (1689)") made William and Mary joint monarchs of England and Ireland. A [separate but similar Scottish settlement](/wiki/Glorious_Revolution_in_Scotland "Glorious Revolution in Scotland") was made in June. During the early years of his reign, William was occupied abroad with the [Nine Years' War](/wiki/Nine_Years%27_War "Nine Years' War") (1688–1697), leaving Mary to govern the three kingdoms alone.

Domestically, the Revolution confirmed the primacy of Parliament over the Crown in both England and Scotland. In terms of external policy, until his death in 1702, William combined the roles of Dutch [stadtholder](/wiki/Stadtholder "Stadtholder") and British monarch. Both states thus became allies in resisting French expansion, an alliance which persisted for much of the 18th century, despite differing objectives.

## Background

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Glorious_Revolution&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Background")]

James became king in 1685 with widespread backing in all three of his kingdoms, despite his conversion to [Roman Catholicism](/wiki/Catholicism "Catholicism") while in exile during the [English Civil War](/wiki/English_Civil_War "English Civil War").[[2]](#cite_note-3) June 1685 even saw James successfully crush Protestant uprisings in both [Scotland](/wiki/Argyll%27s_Rising "Argyll's Rising") and [England](/wiki/Monmouth_Rebellion "Monmouth Rebellion").[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMiller1978124–125-4) However, James failed to appreciate the extent to which his power relied on support from the [landed gentry](/wiki/Landed_gentry "Landed gentry"), whose alienation fatally damaged his regime. The vast majority of the gentry in [England](/wiki/Kingdom_of_England "Kingdom of England") and [Scotland](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Scotland "Kingdom of Scotland") were [Protestant](/wiki/Protestant "Protestant"), while even in largely Catholic [Ireland](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Ireland "Kingdom of Ireland") a disproportionate number were members of the Protestant [Church of Ireland](/wiki/Church_of_Ireland "Church of Ireland"). His supporters accepted James's personal religious beliefs, so long as he maintained the primacy of the Protestant [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England") and [Church of Scotland](/wiki/Church_of_Scotland "Church of Scotland"). When his policies appeared to undermine them, it destabilised all three kingdoms.[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarris199928–30-5)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dd/King_James_II_by_Sir_Godfrey_Kneller%2C_Bt.jpg/250px-King_James_II_by_Sir_Godfrey_Kneller%2C_Bt.jpg)](/wiki/File:King_James_II_by_Sir_Godfrey_Kneller,_Bt.jpg)

James II and VII, King of England, Scotland and Ireland. *[Portrait of James II](/wiki/Portrait_of_James_II_of_England "Portrait of James II of England")* by [Godfrey Kneller](/wiki/Godfrey_Kneller "Godfrey Kneller"), [National Portrait Gallery](/wiki/National_Portrait_Gallery,_London "National Portrait Gallery, London"), 1684

[Stuart political ideology](/wiki/Jacobitism#Ideology "Jacobitism") derived from [James VI and I](/wiki/James_VI_and_I "James VI and I"), whose political ideology was based on the principle of [divine right](/wiki/Divine_right_of_kings "Divine right of kings"), and [Parliament](/wiki/Parliament_of_England "Parliament of England")'s function was to obey.[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStephen201055–58-6) Disputes over the relationship between king and Parliament led to the [War of the Three Kingdoms](/wiki/War_of_the_Three_Kingdoms "War of the Three Kingdoms") and continued after the 1660 [Stuart Restoration](/wiki/Stuart_Restoration "Stuart Restoration"). [Charles II](/wiki/Charles_II_of_England "Charles II of England") came to rely on the [Royal Prerogative](/wiki/Royal_Prerogative "Royal Prerogative") since measures passed in this way could be withdrawn when he decided, rather than Parliament. It could not though be used for major legislation or taxation.[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMiller197844-7)

Concern that Charles II intended to create an [absolute monarchy](/wiki/Absolute_monarchy "Absolute monarchy") led to the 1679 to 1681 [Exclusion Crisis](/wiki/Exclusion_Crisis "Exclusion Crisis"), dividing the English political class into those who wanted to 'exclude' James from the throne, mostly [Whigs](/wiki/British_Whig_Party "British Whig Party"), and their opponents, mostly [Tories](/wiki/Tories_(political_faction) "Tories (political faction)"). However, in 1685 many Whigs feared the consequences of bypassing the 'natural heir', while Tories were often strongly anti-Catholic, and their support assumed the continued primacy of the Church of England. Most importantly, it was seen as a short-term issue; James was 52, his marriage to [Mary of Modena](/wiki/Mary_of_Modena "Mary of Modena") remained childless after 11 years, and the heirs were his Protestant daughters, Mary and [Anne](/wiki/Anne,_Queen_of_Great_Britain "Anne, Queen of Great Britain").[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWormsley2015189-8)

There was much greater sympathy in Scotland for a 'Stuart heir', and the 1681 Succession Act confirmed the duty of all to support him, 'regardless of religion.'[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJackson200338–54-9) Over 95 percent of Scots belonged to the national church or kirk;[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] even other Protestant sects were banned, and by 1680, Catholics were a tiny minority confined to parts of the aristocracy and the remote Highlands.[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBaker2009290–291-10) [Episcopalians](/wiki/Episcopal_polity "Episcopal polity") had regained control of the kirk in 1660, leading to a series of [Presbyterian](/wiki/Presbyterian_polity "Presbyterian polity") uprisings, but memories of the bitter religious conflicts of the Civil War period meant the majority preferred stability.[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarris2006153–155-11)

In England and Scotland, most of those who backed James in 1685 wanted to retain existing political and religious arrangements, but this was not the case in Ireland. While he was guaranteed support from the Catholic majority, James was also popular among Irish Protestants, since the Church of Ireland depended on Royal support for its survival, while [Ulster](/wiki/Ulster "Ulster") was dominated by Presbyterians who supported his tolerance policies. However, religion was only one factor; of equal concern for Catholics were laws barring them from serving in the military or holding public office, and land reform. In 1600, 90% of Irish land was owned by Catholics but following a series of confiscations during the 17th century, this had dropped to 22% in 1685.[[11]](#cite_note-12) Catholic and Protestant merchants in [Dublin](/wiki/Dublin "Dublin") and elsewhere objected to commercial restrictions placing them at a disadvantage to their English competitors.[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarris2006106–108-13)

### The political background in England

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Glorious_Revolution&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: The political background in England")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/25/Revocation_of_the_Edict_of_Nantes.jpg/250px-Revocation_of_the_Edict_of_Nantes.jpg)](/wiki/File:Revocation_of_the_Edict_of_Nantes.jpg)

James's attempts to allow tolerance for English Catholics coincided with the October 1685 [Edict of Fontainebleau](/wiki/Edict_of_Fontainebleau "Edict of Fontainebleau") revoking it for [Huguenots](/wiki/Huguenots "Huguenots").

While James's supporters viewed hereditary succession as more important than his personal Catholicism, they opposed his policies of 'Tolerance' under which Catholics would be allowed to hold public office and engage in public life. Opposition was led by devout [Anglicans](/wiki/Anglicans "Anglicans")[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarris1993124-14) who argued that the measures he proposed were incompatible with the oath he had sworn as king to uphold the supremacy of the Church of England. According to opponents, in demanding that Parliament approve his measures James was not only breaking his own word but requiring others to do the same. Parliament refused to comply, despite being "the most [Loyal Parliament](/wiki/Loyal_Parliament "Loyal Parliament") a [Stuart](/wiki/House_of_Stuart "House of Stuart") ever had".[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWakeling189691-15)

Although historians generally accept James wished to promote Catholicism, not establish an [absolute monarchy](/wiki/Absolute_monarchy "Absolute monarchy"), his stubborn and inflexible reaction to opposition had the same result. When the English and Scottish Parliaments refused to repeal the 1678 and 1681 [Test Acts](/wiki/Test_Act "Test Act"), he suspended them in November 1685 and ruled by decree. Attempts to form a 'King's party' of Catholics, [English Dissenters](/wiki/English_Dissenters "English Dissenters") and dissident Scottish Presbyterians was politically short-sighted, since it rewarded those who joined the 1685 rebellions and undermined his supporters.[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarris2006179–181-16)

Demanding tolerance for Catholics was also badly timed. In October 1685 [Louis XIV](/wiki/Louis_XIV "Louis XIV") issued the [Edict of Fontainebleau](/wiki/Edict_of_Fontainebleau "Edict of Fontainebleau") revoking the 1598 [Edict of Nantes](/wiki/Edict_of_Nantes "Edict of Nantes") which had given [French Protestants](/wiki/Huguenots "Huguenots") the right to practise their religion; over the next four years, an estimated 200,000 to 400,000 went into exile, 40,000 of whom settled in London.[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESpielvogel1980410-17) Combined with Louis's expansionist policies and the killing of 2,000 [Vaudois Protestants](/wiki/Savoyard-Waldensian_Wars "Savoyard-Waldensian Wars") in 1686, it led to fears Protestant Europe was threatened by a Catholic counter-reformation.[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBosher19946–8-18) These concerns were reinforced by events in Ireland; the [Lord Deputy](/wiki/Lord_Deputy "Lord Deputy"), the [Earl of Tyrconnell](/wiki/Richard_Talbot,_1st_Earl_of_Tyrconnell "Richard Talbot, 1st Earl of Tyrconnell"), wanted to create a Catholic establishment able to survive James's death, which meant replacing Protestant officials at a pace that was inherently destabilising.[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarris2006103-19)

### Timeline of events: 1686 to 1688

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Glorious_Revolution&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Timeline of events: 1686 to 1688")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3c/The_Seven_Bishops_committed_to_the_Tower_in_1688_from_NPG.jpg/250px-The_Seven_Bishops_committed_to_the_Tower_in_1688_from_NPG.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Seven_Bishops_committed_to_the_Tower_in_1688_from_NPG.jpg)

The [Seven Bishops](/wiki/Seven_Bishops "Seven Bishops") prosecuted for [seditious libel](/wiki/Seditious_libel "Seditious libel") in 1688

The majority of those who backed James in 1685 did so because they wanted stability and the rule of law, qualities frequently undermined by his actions. After suspending Parliament in November 1685, he sought to rule by decree; although the principle was not disputed, the widening of its scope caused considerable concern, particularly when judges who disagreed with its application were dismissed.[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMiller1978156–157-20) He then alienated many by perceived attacks on the established church; [Henry Compton, Bishop of London](/wiki/Henry_Compton_(bishop) "Henry Compton (bishop)"), was suspended for refusing to ban [John Sharp](/wiki/John_Sharp_(Archbishop_of_York) "John Sharp (Archbishop of York)") from preaching after he gave an anti-Catholic sermon.[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarpenter195696–98-21)

He often made things worse by political clumsiness; to general fury, the [Ecclesiastical Commission of 1686](/wiki/Ecclesiastical_Commission_of_1686 "Ecclesiastical Commission of 1686") established to discipline the Church of England included suspected Catholics like the [Earl of Huntingdon](/wiki/Theophilus_Hastings,_7th_Earl_of_Huntingdon "Theophilus Hastings, 7th Earl of Huntingdon").[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWalker195681-22) This was combined with an inability to accept opposition; in April 1687, he ordered [Magdalen College, Oxford](/wiki/Magdalen_College,_Oxford "Magdalen College, Oxford"), to elect a Catholic sympathiser named [Anthony Farmer](/wiki/Anthony_Farmer "Anthony Farmer") as president, but as he was ineligible under the college statutes, the [fellows](/wiki/Fellow "Fellow") elected [John Hough](/wiki/John_Hough_(bishop) "John Hough (bishop)") instead. Both Farmer and Hough withdrew in favour of another candidate selected by James, who then demanded the fellows personally apologise on their knees for 'defying' him; when they refused, they were replaced by Catholics.[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarris1993130-23)

Attempts to create an alternative 'Kings Party' were never likely to succeed, as English Catholics made up only 1.1% of the population and [Nonconformists](/wiki/Nonconformist_(Protestantism) "Nonconformist (Protestantism)") 4.4%.[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEField2012695-24) Both groups were divided; since private worship was generally tolerated, Catholic moderates feared greater visibility would provoke a backlash. Among Nonconformists, while [Quakers](/wiki/Quakers "Quakers") and [Congregationalists](/wiki/Congregationalists "Congregationalists") supported repeal of the Test Acts, the majority wanted to amend the 1662 Act of Uniformity and be allowed back into the Church of England.[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMiller1978171–172-25) When James ensured the election of the Presbyterian John Shorter as [Lord Mayor of London](/wiki/Lord_Mayor_of_London "Lord Mayor of London") in 1687, Shorter insisted on complying with the Test Act, reportedly because of a "distrust of the King's favour...thus encouraging that which His Majesties whole Endeavours were intended to disannull."[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarris2006235-26)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6c/James_Francis_Edward_Stuart_c._1703_attributed_to_Alexis_Simon_Belle.jpg/250px-James_Francis_Edward_Stuart_c._1703_attributed_to_Alexis_Simon_Belle.jpg)](/wiki/File:James_Francis_Edward_Stuart_c._1703_attributed_to_Alexis_Simon_Belle.jpg)

[James Francis Edward Stuart](/wiki/James_Francis_Edward_Stuart "James Francis Edward Stuart"), c. 1703, whose birth in June 1688 created the possibility of a Catholic dynasty

To ensure a compliant Parliament, James required potential [MPs](/wiki/Member_of_parliament "Member of parliament") to be approved by their local [Lord Lieutenant](/wiki/Lord_Lieutenant "Lord Lieutenant"); eligibility for both offices required positive answers in writing to the 'Three Questions', one being a commitment to repeal of the Test Act.[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMiller1978127–129-27) In addition, local government and town corporations were purged to create an obedient electoral machine, further alienating the county gentry who had formed the majority of those who backed James in 1685.[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJones1988146-28) On 24 August 1688, writs were issued for a general election.[[28]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJones1988150-29)

The expansion of the military caused great concern, particularly in England and Scotland, where memories of the Civil War left huge resistance to [armies](/w/index.php?title=Standing_Standing_army_(Great_Britain)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Standing Standing army (Great Britain) (page does not exist)").[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChilds1987184-30) In Ireland, Talbot replaced Protestant officers with Catholics; James did the same in England, while basing the troops at [Hounslow](/wiki/Hounslow "Hounslow") appeared a deliberate attempt to overawe Parliament.[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChilds198096–97-31) In April 1688, he ordered his [Declaration of Indulgence](/wiki/Declaration_of_Indulgence_(1687) "Declaration of Indulgence (1687)") read in every church; when the [Archbishop of Canterbury](/wiki/William_Sancroft "William Sancroft") and six other bishops refused, they were charged with [seditious libel](/wiki/Seditious_libel "Seditious libel") and confined in the [Tower of London](/wiki/Tower_of_London "Tower of London"). Two events turned dissent into a crisis; the birth of [James Francis Edward Stuart](/wiki/James_Francis_Edward_Stuart "James Francis Edward Stuart") on 10 June created the prospect of a Catholic dynasty, while the acquittal of the [Seven Bishops](/wiki/Seven_Bishops "Seven Bishops") on 30 June destroyed James's political authority.[[31]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarris2006235–236-32)

## Dutch intervention

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Glorious_Revolution&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Dutch intervention")]

### Prelude: 1685 to June 1688

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Glorious_Revolution&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Prelude: 1685 to June 1688")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a3/Hugo_Vogel_-_Empfang_der_Refugies_%28Hugenotten%29_durch_den_Gro%C3%9Fen_Kurf%C3%BCrsten_im_Potsdamer_Schloss%2C_1885.JPG/250px-Hugo_Vogel_-_Empfang_der_Refugies_%28Hugenotten%29_durch_den_Gro%C3%9Fen_Kurf%C3%BCrsten_im_Potsdamer_Schloss%2C_1885.JPG)](/wiki/File:Hugo_Vogel_-_Empfang_der_Refugies_(Hugenotten)_durch_den_Gro%C3%9Fen_Kurf%C3%BCrsten_im_Potsdamer_Schloss,_1885.JPG)

[Huguenot](/wiki/Huguenot "Huguenot") refugees, whose expulsion from France in 1685 helped create a sense that Protestant Europe was under threat

In 1677, James's elder daughter and heir Mary married her Protestant cousin [William III of Orange](/wiki/William_III_of_Orange "William III of Orange"), [stadtholder](/wiki/Stadtholder "Stadtholder") of the main provinces of the [Dutch Republic](/wiki/Dutch_Republic "Dutch Republic"). The two initially shared common objectives in wanting Mary to succeed her father, while French ambitions in the [Spanish Netherlands](/wiki/Spanish_Netherlands "Spanish Netherlands") threatened both English and Dutch trade.[[32]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMiller197881–82-33) Although William sent James [troops](/wiki/Scots_Brigade "Scots Brigade") to help suppress the 1685 [Monmouth Rebellion](/wiki/Monmouth_Rebellion "Monmouth Rebellion"), their relationship deteriorated thereafter.[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETroost2005175-34)

The [Franco-Dutch War](/wiki/Franco-Dutch_War "Franco-Dutch War"), continued [French expansion](/wiki/War_of_the_Reunions "War of the Reunions"), and [expulsion of the Huguenots](/wiki/Edict_of_Fontainebleau "Edict of Fontainebleau") meant William assumed another war was inevitable, and although the [States General of the Netherlands](/wiki/States_General_of_the_Netherlands "States General of the Netherlands") preferred peace, the majority accepted he was correct. This view was widely shared throughout Protestant Europe; in October 1685, [Frederick William, Elector of Brandenburg](/wiki/Frederick_William,_the_Great_Elector "Frederick William, the Great Elector") renounced his French alliance for one with the Dutch. In July 1686, other Protestant states formed the anti-French [League of Augsburg](/wiki/League_of_Augsburg "League of Augsburg"), with Dutch support. Securing or neutralising English resources, especially the [Royal Navy](/wiki/Royal_Navy "Royal Navy"), now became key to both sides.[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStapleton200363–64-35)

Following a skirmish between French and Dutch naval vessels in July 1686, William concluded English neutrality was not enough and he needed their active support in the event of war.[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETroost2001187-36) His relationship with James was affected by the fact both men relied on advisors with relatively limited views; in William's case, mainly English and Scots Presbyterian exiles, the latter with close links to the Protestant minority in Ireland, who saw Tyrconnell's policies as a threat to their existence. Having largely alienated his Tory support base, James depended on a small circle of Catholic converts like [Sunderland](/wiki/Robert_Spencer,_2nd_Earl_of_Sunderland "Robert Spencer, 2nd Earl of Sunderland"), [Melfort](/wiki/John_Drummond,_1st_Earl_of_Melfort "John Drummond, 1st Earl of Melfort") and [Perth](/wiki/James_Drummond,_4th_Earl_of_Perth "James Drummond, 4th Earl of Perth").[[36]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGlozier2000233–234-37)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/aa/Portret_van_Willem_III_%281650-1702%29%2C_prins_van_Oranje%2C_SK-A-879.jpg/250px-Portret_van_Willem_III_%281650-1702%29%2C_prins_van_Oranje%2C_SK-A-879.jpg)](/wiki/File:Portret_van_Willem_III_(1650-1702),_prins_van_Oranje,_SK-A-879.jpg)

[William III of England](/wiki/William_III_of_England "William III of England"), [stadtholder](/wiki/Stadtholder "Stadtholder") of Guelders, Holland, Zealand, Utrecht and Overijssel

Suspicions increased when James sought William's backing for repealing the [Test Acts](/wiki/Test_Acts "Test Acts"); he predictably refused, further damaging their relationship.[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMiller1978213–214-38) Having previously assumed he was guaranteed English support in a war with France, William now worried he might face an Anglo-French alliance like during the *[Rampjaar](/wiki/Rampjaar "Rampjaar")*, despite assurances by James he had no intention of doing so. Historians argue whether these assurances were genuine, but James did not fully appreciate the distrust caused by his domestic policies.[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarris2006256-39) In August 1687, William's cousin [de Zuylestein](/wiki/William_Nassau_de_Zuylestein,_1st_Earl_of_Rochford "William Nassau de Zuylestein, 1st Earl of Rochford") travelled to England with condolences on the death of [Mary of Modena](/wiki/Mary_of_Modena "Mary of Modena")'s mother, allowing him to make contact with the political opposition. Throughout 1688, his English supporters provided William detailed information on public opinion and developments, very little of which was intercepted.[[39]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJones1988222-40)

In October 1687, after fourteen years of marriage and multiple miscarriages, it was announced the Queen was pregnant, Melfort immediately declaring it was a boy. When James then wrote to Mary urging her to convert to Catholicism, it convinced many he was seeking a Catholic heir, one way or the other and may have been a deciding factor in whether to invade.[[40]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHoak199624-41) Early in 1688, [a pamphlet](/wiki/A_letter,_writ_by_Mijn_Heer_Fagel_(Pensioner_of_Holland)_to_Mr._James_Stewart_(Advocate);_giving_an_account_of_the_Prince_and_Princess_of_Orange%27s_thoughts_concerning_the_repeal_of_the_test,_and_the_penal_laws "A letter, writ by Mijn Heer Fagel (Pensioner of Holland) to Mr. James Stewart (Advocate); giving an account of the Prince and Princess of Orange's thoughts concerning the repeal of the test, and the penal laws") circulated in England written by Dutch [Grand Pensionary](/wiki/Grand_Pensionary "Grand Pensionary") [Gaspar Fagel](/wiki/Gaspar_Fagel "Gaspar Fagel"); this guaranteed William's support for freedom of worship for Dissenters *and* retaining the Test Acts, unlike James who offered tolerance in return for repeal.[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarris2006256–257-42)[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFagel1688-43)

In April 1688, [Louis XIV](/wiki/Louis_XIV "Louis XIV") announced tariffs on Dutch [herring](/wiki/Herring "Herring") imports, along with plans to support the Royal Navy in the [English Channel](/wiki/English_Channel "English Channel"). James immediately denied making any such request, but fearing it was the prelude to a formal alliance, the Dutch began preparing a military intervention.[[43]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETroost2005191-44) On the pretext of needing additional resources to deal with [French privateers](/wiki/Dunkirkers "Dunkirkers"), in July the States General authorised an additional 9,000 sailors and 21 new warships.[[44]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPrud'homme_van_Reine2009287-45)

### Invitation to William

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Glorious_Revolution&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Invitation to William")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/24/Earl_of_Romney.jpg/250px-Earl_of_Romney.jpg)](/wiki/File:Earl_of_Romney.jpg)

[Henry Sydney](/wiki/Henry_Sydney,_1st_Earl_of_Romney "Henry Sydney, 1st Earl of Romney"), who drafted the [Invitation to William](/wiki/Invitation_to_William "Invitation to William")

The success of William's invasion would partly depend on domestic support, and at the end of April William met with [Edward Russell](/wiki/Edward_Russell,_1st_Earl_of_Orford "Edward Russell, 1st Earl of Orford"), unofficial envoy for the Whig opposition. In a conversation recorded by [Gilbert Burnet](/wiki/Gilbert_Burnet "Gilbert Burnet"), he requested a formal invitation asking him to "rescue the nation and the religion", with a projected date of end September.[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBaxter1966225-46) William subsequently claimed he was 'forced' to take control of the conspiracy when Russell warned him the English would rise against James even without his help, and he feared this would lead to a republic, depriving his wife of her inheritance.[[46]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBaxter1966231-47)

Although this version is strongly disputed, Zuylestein returned to England in June, ostensibly to congratulate James on his new son, in reality to co-ordinate with William's supporters.[[47]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJones1988238–239-48) Spurred by the prospect of a Catholic successor, the "[Invitation to William](/wiki/Invitation_to_William "Invitation to William")" was quickly drafted by [Henry Sydney](/wiki/Henry_Sydney,_1st_Earl_of_Romney "Henry Sydney, 1st Earl of Romney"), later described by Whig historians as "the great wheel on which the Revolution rolled".[[39]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJones1988222-40)[[b]](#cite_note-50) The signatories provided no considerable political power, but they were selected to make it seem like they represented a broad spectrum, and provided William with an essential propaganda tool.[[49]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEIsrael200312-51)[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChilds1988418-52)

The signatories were:

* [Henry Sydney](/wiki/Henry_Sydney,_1st_Earl_of_Romney "Henry Sydney, 1st Earl of Romney") (who wrote the letter)
* [Edward Russell](/wiki/Edward_Russell,_1st_Earl_of_Orford "Edward Russell, 1st Earl of Orford")
* [Charles Talbot, 12th Earl of Shrewsbury](/wiki/Charles_Talbot,_1st_Duke_of_Shrewsbury "Charles Talbot, 1st Duke of Shrewsbury")
* [William Cavendish, 4th Earl of Devonshire](/wiki/William_Cavendish,_1st_Duke_of_Devonshire "William Cavendish, 1st Duke of Devonshire")
* [Thomas Osborne, 1st Earl of Danby](/wiki/Thomas_Osborne,_1st_Duke_of_Leeds "Thomas Osborne, 1st Duke of Leeds")
* [Richard Lumley, 2nd Viscount Lumley](/wiki/Richard_Lumley,_1st_Earl_of_Scarbrough "Richard Lumley, 1st Earl of Scarbrough")
* [Henry Compton](/wiki/Henry_Compton_(bishop) "Henry Compton (bishop)"), [Bishop of London](/wiki/Bishop_of_London "Bishop of London")

[Danby](/wiki/Thomas_Osborne,_1st_Duke_of_Leeds "Thomas Osborne, 1st Duke of Leeds"), a Tory, and [Devonshire](/wiki/William_Cavendish,_1st_Duke_of_Devonshire "William Cavendish, 1st Duke of Devonshire"), a Whig; Henry Compton, Bishop of London, for the church; [Shrewsbury](/wiki/Charles_Talbot,_Duke_of_Shrewsbury "Charles Talbot, Duke of Shrewsbury") and [Lumley](/wiki/Richard_Lumley,_1st_Earl_of_Scarbrough "Richard Lumley, 1st Earl of Scarbrough") for the army, and finally Russell and Sydney for the navy.[[51]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarris2006271-53) They promised to support a Dutch landing, but stressed the importance of acting quickly.[[52]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarris2006272-54)

The Invitation was carried to The Hague on 30 June by [Rear Admiral Herbert](/wiki/Arthur_Herbert,_1st_Earl_of_Torrington "Arthur Herbert, 1st Earl of Torrington"), disguised as a common sailor. Meanwhile, William's ally [Bentinck](/wiki/William_Bentinck,_1st_Earl_of_Portland "William Bentinck, 1st Earl of Portland") launched a propaganda campaign in England, which presented him as a "true Stuart", but one without the faults of either James or Charles II. Much of the "spontaneous" support for William on his landing was organised by Bentinck and his agents.[[53]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEClaydonLevillain2016150-55)

## Dutch preparations: July to September 1688

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Glorious_Revolution&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Dutch preparations: July to September 1688")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/39/1672_Dutch_War.jpg/250px-1672_Dutch_War.jpg)](/wiki/File:1672_Dutch_War.jpg)

The Dutch were concerned by their vulnerable eastern border. In 1672, an alliance with the [Electorate of Cologne](/wiki/Electorate_of_Cologne "Electorate of Cologne") allowed France to nearly over-run the Republic.

William's key strategic purpose was creating a defensive coalition that would block further French expansion in Europe, an objective not shared by the majority of his English supporters. In 1672, an alliance with the [Electorate of Cologne](/wiki/Electorate_of_Cologne "Electorate of Cologne") had enabled France to bypass Dutch forward defences and [nearly over-run](/wiki/Rampjaar "Rampjaar") the Republic, so ensuring an anti-French ruler was vital to prevent a repetition. As an [ecclesiastical principality](/wiki/Hochstift "Hochstift") of the Holy Roman Empire, Cologne's ruler was nominated by [Pope Innocent XI](/wiki/Pope_Innocent_XI "Pope Innocent XI"), in conjunction with [Emperor Leopold I](/wiki/Emperor_Leopold_I "Emperor Leopold I").[[54]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMiller1978153-56) Both Louis and James were in dispute with Innocent over the right to appoint Catholic bishops and clergy; when the old Elector died in June 1688, Innocent and Leopold ignored the French candidate in favour of [Joseph Clemens of Bavaria](/wiki/Joseph_Clemens_of_Bavaria "Joseph Clemens of Bavaria").[[55]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYoung2004251–252-57)

After 1678, France continued its expansion into the [Rhineland](/wiki/Rhineland "Rhineland"), including the 1683 to 1684 [War of the Reunions](/wiki/War_of_the_Reunions "War of the Reunions"), additional territorial demands in the [Palatinate](/wiki/Electoral_Palatinate "Electoral Palatinate"), and construction of forts at [Landau](/wiki/Landau "Landau") and [Traben-Trarbach](/wiki/Traben-Trarbach "Traben-Trarbach").[[56]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDuffy199520-58) This presented an existential threat to Habsburg dominance, guaranteeing Leopold's support for the Dutch, and negating French attempts to build German alliances.[[57]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETroost2005198-59) William's envoy Johann von Görtz assured Leopold English Catholics would not be persecuted and intervention was to elect a free Parliament, not depose James, a convenient fiction that allowed him to remain neutral.[[58]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYoung2004255-60)

Although his English supporters considered a token force sufficient, William assembled 260 transport ships and 15,000 men, nearly half the 30,000 strong [Dutch States Army](/wiki/Dutch_States_Army "Dutch States Army"). With France on the verge of war, their absence was of great concern to the States General and Bentinck hired 13,616 German mercenaries to man Dutch border fortresses, freeing elite units like the [Scots Brigade](/wiki/Scots_Brigade "Scots Brigade") for use in England.[[59]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJardine200838-61) The increase could be presented as a limited precaution against French aggression, as the Dutch would typically double or triple their army strength in wartime;[[c]](#cite_note-63) William instructed his experienced deputy [Schomberg](/wiki/Frederick_Schomberg,_1st_Duke_of_Schomberg "Frederick Schomberg, 1st Duke of Schomberg") to prepare for a campaign in Germany.[[61]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBaxter1966232–233-64)

## Decision to invade

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Glorious_Revolution&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Decision to invade")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3a/Abraham_Hulk_-_Fishing_Boats_by_the_Coast.jpg/250px-Abraham_Hulk_-_Fishing_Boats_by_the_Coast.jpg)](/wiki/File:Abraham_Hulk_-_Fishing_Boats_by_the_Coast.jpg)

A Dutch herring fleet. French tariffs on this lucrative trade helped William build domestic support for military intervention.

At the beginning of September, an invasion remained in the balance, with the States General fearing a French attack via [Flanders](/wiki/Flanders "Flanders") while their army was in England. However, the surrender of [Belgrade](/wiki/Siege_of_Belgrade_(1688) "Siege of Belgrade (1688)") on 6 September seemed to presage an Ottoman collapse and release Austrian resources for use in Germany. Hoping to act before Leopold could respond and relieve pressure on the Ottomans, Louis attacked [Philippsburg](/wiki/Siege_of_Philippsburg_(1688) "Siege of Philippsburg (1688)"). With France now committed in Germany, this greatly reduced the threat to the Dutch.[[62]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcKayScott198441-65)

Instead, Louis attempted to intimidate the States General, and on 9 September, his envoy [D'Avaux](/wiki/Jean-Antoine_de_Mesmes_(diplomat) "Jean-Antoine de Mesmes (diplomat)") handed them two letters. The first warned an attack on James meant war with France, the second any interference with French operations in Germany would end with the destruction of the Dutch state.[[63]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJardine200841-66) Both misfired; convinced Louis was trying to drag him into war, James told the Dutch there was no secret Anglo-French alliance against them, although his denials only increased their suspicions. By confirming France's primary objective was the Rhineland, the second allowed William to move troops from the eastern border to the coast, even though most of the new mercenaries had yet to arrive.[[64]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMiller1978178–179-67)

On 22 September, the French seized over 100 Dutch ships, many owned by Amsterdam merchants; in response, on 26 September the Amsterdam City Council agreed to back William.[[65]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJardine200839–40-68) This was a significant decision since the Council dominated the [States of Holland](/wiki/States_of_Holland "States of Holland"), the most powerful political body in the Dutch Republic which contributed nearly 60% of its budget. French troops entered the Rhineland on 27 September and in a secret session held on 29th, William argued for a [pre-emptive strike](/wiki/Pre-emptive_strike "Pre-emptive strike"), as Louis and James would "attempt to bring this state to its ultimate ruin and subjugation, as soon as they find the occasion". This was accepted by the States, with the objective left deliberately vague, other than making the English "King and Nation live in a good relation, and useful to their friends and allies, and especially to this State".[[66]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJardine200837-69)

Following their approval, the Amsterdam financial market raised a loan of four million guilders in only three days, with further financing coming from various sources, including two million guilders from the banker [Francisco Lopes Suasso](/wiki/Francisco_Lopes_Suasso "Francisco Lopes Suasso").[[67]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJardine200852-70)[[d]](#cite_note-72) The biggest concern for Holland was the potential impact on the Dutch economy and politics of William becoming ruler of England; the claim he had no intention of "removing the King from the throne" was not believed. These fears were arguably justified; William's access to English resources permanently diminished Amsterdam's power within the Republic and its status as the world's leading commercial and financial centre.[[69]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETroost2016206–207-73)

## English defensive strategy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Glorious_Revolution&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: English defensive strategy")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6e/George_Legge%2C_1st_Baron_Dartmouth_by_John_Riley.jpg/250px-George_Legge%2C_1st_Baron_Dartmouth_by_John_Riley.jpg)](/wiki/File:George_Legge,_1st_Baron_Dartmouth_by_John_Riley.jpg)

Admiral [Dartmouth](/wiki/George_Legge,_1st_Baron_Dartmouth "George Legge, 1st Baron Dartmouth"), commander of the English fleet

Neither James nor Sunderland trusted Louis, correctly suspecting that his support would continue only so long as it coincided with French interests, while Mary of Modena claimed his warnings were simply an attempt to drag England into an unwanted alliance.[[70]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMiller1978194-74) As a former naval commander, James appreciated the difficulties of a successful invasion, even in good weather, and as autumn approached, the likelihood seemed to diminish. With the Dutch on the verge of war with France, he did not believe the States General would allow William to make the attempt; if they did, his army and navy were strong enough to defeat it.[[71]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMiller1978195-75)

Reasonable in theory, his reliance on the loyalty and efficiency of the military proved deeply flawed. Both the army and the navy remained overwhelmingly Protestant and anti-Catholic; in July, only personal intervention by James prevented a naval mutiny when a Catholic captain held [Mass](/wiki/Mass_(liturgy) "Mass (liturgy)") on his ship. The transfer of 2,500 Catholics from the [Royal Irish Army](/wiki/Royal_Irish_Army "Royal Irish Army") to England in September led to clashes with Protestant troops, some of his most reliable units refused to obey orders, and many of their officers resigned.[[72]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMiller1978196-76)

When James demanded the repatriation of all six regiments of the Scots Brigade in January 1688,[[73]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETroost2001196-77) William refused but used the opportunity to purge those considered unreliable, a total of 104 officers and 44 soldiers.[[74]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChilds198461-78) Some may have been Williamite agents, such as [Colonel Belasyse](/wiki/Henry_Belasyse_(died_1717) "Henry Belasyse (died 1717)"), a Protestant with over 15 years of service who returned to his family estates in [Yorkshire](/wiki/Yorkshire "Yorkshire") and made contact with Danby. The promotion of Catholic former Brigade officers like [Thomas Buchan](/wiki/Thomas_Buchan "Thomas Buchan") and [Alexander Cannon](/wiki/Alexander_Cannon_(general) "Alexander Cannon (general)") to command positions led to the formation of the Association of Protestant Officers, which included senior veterans like [Charles Trelawny](/wiki/Charles_Trelawny "Charles Trelawny"), [John Churchill](/wiki/John_Churchill "John Churchill"), and [Percy Kirke](/wiki/Percy_Kirke "Percy Kirke").[[75]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHolmes2009136-79)

On 14 August, Churchill offered his support to William, helping convince him it was safe to risk an invasion; although James was aware of the conspiracy, he took no action.[[76]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETroost2005195–196-80) One reason may have been fears over the impact on the army; with a notional strength of 34,000, it looked impressive on paper but morale was brittle while many were untrained or lacked weapons. It also had to fill policing roles previously delegated to the militia, which had been deliberately allowed to decay; most of the 4,000 regular troops brought from Scotland in October had to be stationed in London to keep order. In October, attempts were made to restore the militia but many members were reportedly so angry at the changes made to local corporations, James was advised it was better not to raise them.[[77]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMiller1973671–672-81)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/62/Thomas_Osborne%2C_1st_Duke_of_Leeds_by_Sir_Peter_Lely.jpg/250px-Thomas_Osborne%2C_1st_Duke_of_Leeds_by_Sir_Peter_Lely.jpg)](/wiki/File:Thomas_Osborne,_1st_Duke_of_Leeds_by_Sir_Peter_Lely.jpg)

[Lord Danby](/wiki/Thomas_Osborne,_1st_Duke_of_Leeds "Thomas Osborne, 1st Duke of Leeds"), one of the Immortal Seven and William's agent in Northern England

Widespread discontent and growing hostility to the Stuart regime were particularly apparent in North-East and South-West England, the two landing places identified by William. A Tory whose brother [Jonathan](/wiki/Sir_Jonathan_Trelawny,_3rd_Baronet "Sir Jonathan Trelawny, 3rd Baronet") was one of the Seven Bishops, Trelawny's commitment confirmed support from a powerful and well-connected [West Country](/wiki/West_Country "West Country") bloc, allowing access to the ports of [Plymouth](/wiki/Plymouth "Plymouth") and Torbay. In the north, a force organised by Belasyse and Danby prepared to seize [York](/wiki/York "York"), its most important city, and [Hull](/wiki/Kingston_upon_Hull "Kingston upon Hull"), its largest port.[[78]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarris2006285-82)

Herbert had been replaced by [Dartmouth](/wiki/George_Legge,_1st_Baron_Dartmouth "George Legge, 1st Baron Dartmouth") as commander of the fleet when he defected in June but many captains owed him their appointments and were of doubtful loyalty. Dartmouth suspected Berkeley and [Grafton](/wiki/Henry_FitzRoy,_1st_Duke_of_Grafton "Henry FitzRoy, 1st Duke of Grafton") of plotting to overthrow him; to monitor them, he placed their ships next to his and minimised contact between the other vessels to prevent conspiracy.[[79]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDavies2004-83) A lack of funds meant that excluding fireships and light scouting vessels, only 16 warships were available in early October, all [third rates](/wiki/Third_rate "Third rate") or [fourth rates](/wiki/Fourth_rate "Fourth rate") that were short of both men and supplies.[[80]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurchett170314–17-84)

While [the Downs](/wiki/The_Downs_(ship_anchorage) "The Downs (ship anchorage)") was the best place to intercept a cross-Channel attack, it was also vulnerable to a surprise assault, even for ships fully crewed and adequately provisioned. Instead, James placed his ships in a strong defensive position near [Chatham Dockyard](/wiki/Chatham_Historic_Dockyard "Chatham Historic Dockyard"), believing the Dutch would seek to establish naval superiority before committing to a landing.[[81]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERodger2004138-85) While this had been the original plan, winter storms meant conditions deteriorated rapidly for those on the transports; William therefore decided to sail in convoy and avoid battle.[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPrud'homme_van_Reine2009291-86) The easterly winds that allowed the Dutch to cross prevented the Royal Navy leaving the [Thames](/wiki/Thames "Thames") estuary and intervening.[[81]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERodger2004138-85)

The English fleet was outnumbered 2:1, undercrewed, short of supplies, and in the wrong place. Key landing locations in the south-west and Yorkshire had been secured by sympathisers, while both army and navy were led by officers whose loyalty was questionable. Even early in 1686, foreign observers doubted the military would fight for James against a Protestant heir, and William claimed only to be securing the inheritance of his wife Mary. While still a dangerous undertaking, the invasion was less risky than it seemed.[[83]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMiller1973679-87)

## Invasion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Glorious_Revolution&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Invasion")]

### Embarkation of the army and the Declaration of The Hague

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Glorious_Revolution&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Embarkation of the army and the Declaration of The Hague")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/af/William_III_Landing_at_Brixham%2C_Torbay%2C_5_November_1688.jpg/250px-William_III_Landing_at_Brixham%2C_Torbay%2C_5_November_1688.jpg)](/wiki/File:William_III_Landing_at_Brixham,_Torbay,_5_November_1688.jpg)

William III by [Jan Wyck](/wiki/Jan_Wyck "Jan Wyck"), commemorating the landing at Brixham, Torbay, 5 November 1688

The Dutch preparations, though carried out with great speed, could not remain secret. The English envoy [Ignatius White](/wiki/Ignatius_White "Ignatius White"), the Marquess d'Albeville, warned his country: "an absolute conquest is intended under the specious and ordinary pretences of religion, liberty, property and a free Parliament". Louis threatened an immediate declaration of war if William proceeded and sent James 300,000 livres.[[84]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWestern1972259-88)

Embarkations, begun on 22 September ([Gregorian calendar](/wiki/Gregorian_calendar "Gregorian calendar")), had been completed on 8 October, and the expedition was that day openly approved by the States of Holland; the same day James issued a proclamation to the English nation that it should prepare for a Dutch invasion to ward off conquest. On 30 September/10 October ([Julian](/wiki/Julian_calendar "Julian calendar")/Gregorian calendars) William issued the *Declaration of The Hague* (actually written by Fagel), of which 60,000 copies of the English translation by Gilbert Burnet were distributed after the landing in England,[[85]](#cite_note-89) in which he assured that his only aim was to maintain the Protestant religion, install a free parliament and investigate the legitimacy of the Prince of Wales. He would respect the position of James.[[e]](#cite_note-91)

William went on to condemn James's advisers for overturning the religion, laws, and liberties of England, Scotland, and Ireland by the use of the suspending and dispensing power; the establishment of the "manifestly illegal" commission for ecclesiastical causes and its use to suspend the [Bishop of London](/wiki/Bishop_of_London "Bishop of London") and to remove the Fellows of [Magdalen College, Oxford](/wiki/Magdalen_College,_Oxford "Magdalen College, Oxford"). William also condemned James's attempt to repeal the [Test Acts](/wiki/Test_Act "Test Act") and the penal laws through pressuring individuals and waging an assault on parliamentary boroughs, as well as his purging of the judiciary. James's attempt to pack Parliament was in danger of removing "the last and great remedy for all those evils".[[87]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESpeck198974–75-92)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b6/Het_oorlogsschip_Brielle_op_de_Maas_voor_Rotterdam_Rijksmuseum_SK-A-2539.jpeg/250px-Het_oorlogsschip_Brielle_op_de_Maas_voor_Rotterdam_Rijksmuseum_SK-A-2539.jpeg)](/wiki/File:Het_oorlogsschip_Brielle_op_de_Maas_voor_Rotterdam_Rijksmuseum_SK-A-2539.jpeg)

William boarding *Den Briel*

"Therefore", William continued, "we have thought fit to go over to England, and to carry over with us a force sufficient, by the blessing of God, to defend us from the violence of those evil Counsellors ... this our Expedition is intended for no other design, but to have, a free and lawful Parliament assembled as soon as is possible".[[87]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESpeck198974–75-92) On [4/14](/wiki/Old_Style_and_New_Style_dates "Old Style and New Style dates") October, William responded to the allegations by James in a second declaration, denying any intention to become king or to conquer England, a claim which remains controversial.[[88]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETroost2001199-93)

The swiftness of the embarkations surprised all foreign observers. Louis had in fact delayed his threats against the Dutch until early September because he assumed it then would be too late in the season to set the expedition in motion anyway, if their reaction proved negative; typically, such an enterprise would take at least some months.[[89]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERodger2004137-94) Being ready after the last week of September / first week of October would normally have meant that the Dutch could have profited from the last spell of good weather, as the autumn storms tend to begin in the third week of that month. However, this year they came early. For three weeks, the invasion fleet was prevented by adverse south-westerly gales from departing from the naval port of [Hellevoetsluis](/wiki/Hellevoetsluis "Hellevoetsluis") and Catholics all over the Netherlands and the British kingdoms held prayer sessions that this "popish wind" might endure. However, on [14/24](/wiki/Old_Style_and_New_Style_dates "Old Style and New Style dates") October, it became the famous "[Protestant Wind](/wiki/Protestant_Wind "Protestant Wind")" by turning to the east.[[90]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJones1973201–221-95)

Although most of the warships were provided by the [Admiralty of Amsterdam](/wiki/Admiralty_of_Amsterdam "Admiralty of Amsterdam"), officially the States General treated the operation as a private affair, allowing William use of the [Dutch States Navy](/wiki/Dutch_States_Navy "Dutch States Navy") and army.[[57]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETroost2005198-59) The nominal commander was [Arthur Herbert](/wiki/Arthur_Herbert,_1st_Earl_of_Torrington "Arthur Herbert, 1st Earl of Torrington"), but operational control remained with [Lieutenant-Admiral](/wiki/Lieutenant-Admiral "Lieutenant-Admiral") [Cornelis Evertsen the Youngest](/wiki/Cornelis_Evertsen_the_Youngest "Cornelis Evertsen the Youngest") and Vice-Admiral [Philips van Almonde](/wiki/Philips_van_Almonde "Philips van Almonde").[[95]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPrud'homme_van_Reine2009288-100) Accompanied by [Willem Bastiaensz Schepers](/wiki/Willem_Bastiaensz_Schepers "Willem Bastiaensz Schepers"), the Rotterdam shipping magnate who provided financing, William boarded the [frigate](/wiki/Frigate "Frigate") *Den Briel* on [16/26](/wiki/Old_Style_and_New_Style_dates "Old Style and New Style dates") October.[[96]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJardine200810–11-101)[[97]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBander2014276-102)

With over 400 ships of various types carrying 40,000 men, the expeditionary force was the largest fleet assembled in European waters up to that date.[[98]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENolan2008177-103)[[f]](#cite_note-105) After departing on 19/29 October, the fleet was scattered by a gale, forcing the *Briel* back to [Hellevoetsluis](/wiki/Hellevoetsluis "Hellevoetsluis") on 21/31 October. William refused to go ashore, and the fleet reassembled, having lost only one ship but nearly a thousand horses; press reports deliberately exaggerated the damage and claimed the expedition might be postponed until next spring.[[100]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPrud'homme_van_Reine2009290–291-106)

Dartmouth and his senior commanders considered taking advantage of this by blockading Hellevoetsluis, then decided against it, partly because the stormy weather made it dangerous but also because they could not rely on their men.[[101]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarris2006203-107) William replaced his losses and departed when the wind changed on 1/11 November, this time heading for [Harwich](/wiki/Harwich "Harwich") where Bentinck had prepared a landing site. It has been suggested this was a feint to divert some of Dartmouth's ships north, which proved to be the case and when the wind shifted again, the Dutch armada sailed south into the [Strait of Dover](/wiki/Strait_of_Dover "Strait of Dover").[[100]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPrud'homme_van_Reine2009290–291-106) In doing so, they twice passed the English fleet, which was unable to intercept because of the adverse winds and tides.[[102]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERodger2004139-108)

With the French fleet absent in the Mediterranean,[[89]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERodger2004137-94) on 3/13 November, the fleet entered the [English Channel](/wiki/English_Channel "English Channel") in a formation 25 ships deep. Intended to awe observers with its size and power, the troops were lined up on deck, firing musket volleys, colours flying and [military bands](/wiki/Military_band "Military band") playing. The same wind blowing the Dutch down the Channel trapped Dartmouth in the Thames estuary, leaving him unable to prevent William reaching Torbay on 5 November.[[103]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMiller1978199-109)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/98/Landing_of_William_III_at_Torbay%2C_5_November_1688_RMG_BHC0326.tiff/lossy-page1-250px-Landing_of_William_III_at_Torbay%2C_5_November_1688_RMG_BHC0326.tiff.jpg)](/wiki/File:Landing_of_William_III_at_Torbay,_5_November_1688_RMG_BHC0326.tiff)

The Dutch fleet at Torbay

Bad weather obliged Dartmouth to anchor in Portsmouth for two days, allowing William to complete his disembarkation undisturbed.[[104]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERodger2004137–139-110) Most estimates suggest his force consisted of around 15,000 regular troops,[[g]](#cite_note-113) plus up to 5,000 volunteers, mostly British exiles and French [Huguenots](/wiki/Huguenot "Huguenot").[[107]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEIsrael1995849-114)[[h]](#cite_note-115) He also brought weapons to equip another 20,000 men, although the subsequent collapse of James's army meant most local volunteers were quickly sent home.[[108]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarris2006283-116)

## The collapse of James's rule

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Glorious_Revolution&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: The collapse of James's rule")]

[![Glorious Revolution is located in Southern England](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e7/England_south_location_map.svg/330px-England_south_location_map.svg.png)](/wiki/File:England_south_location_map.svg "Glorious Revolution is located in Southern England")
