Key locations in November 1688

James had considered himself safe from invasion, due to the French threat to the Spanish Netherlands, his navy and because it was late in the year for launching an expedition.[[109]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChilds1980177—178-117) He now panicked and met with the bishops on 28 September, offering them concessions. They responded five days later with demands that he restore the religious position to that prevailing in February 1685, and hold free elections for a new Parliament. Although they hoped this would allow James to remain king, in reality there was little chance of this. At a minimum, he would have to disinherit his son, enforce the Test Acts, and accept the supremacy of Parliament, all of which were unacceptable. In addition, by now his Whig opponents did not trust him to keep his promises, while Tories like Danby were too committed to William to escape punishment.[[110]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMiller1978204-118)

Although his veterans were superior to the largely untested recruits of the Royal Army, William and his English supporters preferred to avoid bloodshed. Torbay was sufficiently far from London to provide time for the regime to collapse on its own, while to avoid alienating the local population, his troops were well supplied and paid three months in advance. On 9 November, he entered [Exeter](/wiki/Exeter "Exeter") and issued a proclamation claiming he sought only to secure the rights of his wife and a free Parliament. Despite this, there was little enthusiasm for either party, and the general mood was one of confusion and distrust.[[111]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJardine200815–16-119) In Northern England, much of the gentry confirmed their backing for the invasion after Danby had the *Declaration* read out in York on 12 November.[[112]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJardine200831–32-120)

On 19 November, James joined his main force of 19,000 at [Salisbury](/wiki/Salisbury "Salisbury"),[[78]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarris2006285-82) but morale was low and the loyalty of some commanders doubtful, with a number of officers defecting to William between 10 and 20 November.[[113]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChilds1980187-121)[[114]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPinkman1954138_&_160-122)[[115]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDillon2007164-123) Although the numbers were relatively small, the defections badly impacted morale,[[113]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChilds1980187-121)[[116]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESpeck198986-124) while the Royal Army was short of both food and ammunition. On 20 November, Royal dragoons clashed with Williamite scouts at [Wincanton](/wiki/Wincanton_Skirmish "Wincanton Skirmish"), but along with a minor skirmish at [Reading](/wiki/Battle_of_Reading_(1688) "Battle of Reading (1688)") on 9 December, these were the only substantial military actions of the campaign. After securing his rear by taking [Plymouth](/wiki/Plymouth "Plymouth") on 18 November, William began his advance on 21 November, while Danby and Belasyse captured York and Hull several days later.[[78]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarris2006285-82)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/73/John_Churchill%2C_1st_Duke_of_Marlborough_by_John_Closterman.jpg/250px-John_Churchill%2C_1st_Duke_of_Marlborough_by_John_Closterman.jpg)](/wiki/File:John_Churchill,_1st_Duke_of_Marlborough_by_John_Closterman.jpg)

[John Churchill](/wiki/John_Churchill "John Churchill"), c. 1685, whose defection to William was a serious blow

His commander [Feversham](/wiki/Louis_de_Duras,_2nd_Earl_of_Feversham "Louis de Duras, 2nd Earl of Feversham") and other senior officers advised James to retreat. Lacking information on William's movements, suspicious of his own soldiers, worn out by lack of sleep and debilitating nose-bleeds, on 23 November James agreed.[[117]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMiller1978201–202-125) Because of its strategic implications, the withdrawal was a practical admission of defeat.[[118]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChilds1980189-126) On 24 November, Churchill, Grafton and [Princess Anne](/wiki/Anne,_Queen_of_Great_Britain "Anne, Queen of Great Britain")'s husband [George](/wiki/Prince_George_of_Denmark "Prince George of Denmark") deserted to William, followed by Anne herself on 26 November. The next day, James met with those peers still in London, and with the exception of Melfort, Perth and other Catholics, they urged him to announce free Parliamentary elections and negotiate with William.[[119]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarris2006284-127)

On 8 December, [Halifax](/wiki/George_Savile,_1st_Marquess_of_Halifax "George Savile, 1st Marquess of Halifax"), [Nottingham](/wiki/Daniel_Finch,_2nd_Earl_of_Nottingham "Daniel Finch, 2nd Earl of Nottingham") and [Godolphin](/wiki/Sidney_Godolphin,_1st_Earl_of_Godolphin "Sidney Godolphin, 1st Earl of Godolphin") met with William at [Hungerford](/wiki/Hungerford "Hungerford") to hear his demands, which included the dismissal of Catholics from public office and funding for his army. Many viewed these as a reasonable basis for a settlement, but James decided to flee the country, convinced his life was threatened. This suggestion is generally dismissed by historians, since William made it clear he would not allow his uncle to be harmed. Some Tories hoped James could retain his throne but name a Protestant successor, while the Whigs preferred to drive him out of the country by imposing conditions he would refuse.[[110]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMiller1978204-118)

The Queen and Prince of Wales left for France on 9 December, James following separately on 10 December.[[120]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMiller1978205-128) Accompanied only by [Edward Hales](/wiki/Sir_Edward_Hales,_3rd_Baronet "Sir Edward Hales, 3rd Baronet") and Ralph Sheldon, he made his way to [Faversham](/wiki/Faversham "Faversham") in Kent seeking passage to France, first dropping the [Great Seal](/wiki/Great_Seal_of_the_Realm "Great Seal of the Realm") in the [River Thames](/wiki/River_Thames "River Thames") in a last-ditch attempt to prevent Parliament being summoned.[[121]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJardine200817-129) In London, his flight and [rumours](/wiki/Irish_Fright "Irish Fright") of a "Papist" invasion led to riots and destruction of Catholic property, which quickly spread throughout the country. To fill the power vacuum, the [Earl of Rochester](/wiki/Laurence_Hyde,_1st_Earl_of_Rochester "Laurence Hyde, 1st Earl of Rochester") set up a temporary government including members of the [Privy Council](/wiki/Privy_Council "Privy Council") and [City of London](/wiki/City_of_London "City of London") authorities, but it took them two days to restore order.[[122]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarris2006296–300-130)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9d/Atlas_Van_der_Hagen-KW1049B11_021-Receptie_van_S.K.H._den_Prince_van_Orange_op_zijn_intrede_tot_London_%3D_THE_Reception_of_His_Royal_Highnesse_the_Prince_of_Orange_at_his_entring_London.jpeg/330px-thumbnail.jpeg)](/wiki/File:Atlas_Van_der_Hagen-KW1049B11_021-Receptie_van_S.K.H._den_Prince_van_Orange_op_zijn_intrede_tot_London_%3D_THE_Reception_of_His_Royal_Highnesse_the_Prince_of_Orange_at_his_entring_London.jpeg)

The entrance of William in London, 16 December 1688

When news arrived, James had been captured in Faversham on 11 December by local fishermen, [Lord Ailesbury](/wiki/Thomas_Bruce,_2nd_Earl_of_Ailesbury "Thomas Bruce, 2nd Earl of Ailesbury"), one of his personal attendants, was sent to escort him back to London. On entering the city on 16 December, he was welcomed by cheering crowds. By making it seem that James remained in control, Tory loyalists hoped for a settlement which would leave them in government. To create an appearance of normality, he heard Mass and presided over a meeting of the Privy Council.[[123]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTE''The_London_Gazette''16882-131)[[i]](#cite_note-132) James made it clear to the French ambassador [Paul Barillon](/wiki/Paul_Barillon "Paul Barillon") that he still intended to escape to France. His few remaining supporters viewed his flight as cowardice, and a failure to ensure law and order criminally negligent.[[124]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMiller1978208-133)

Happy to help him into exile, William recommended he relocate to [Ham](/wiki/Ham,_London "Ham, London"), largely because it was easy to escape from. James suggested [Rochester](/wiki/Rochester,_Kent "Rochester, Kent") instead, allegedly because his personal guard was there, in reality conveniently positioned for a ship to France. On 18 December, he left London with a Dutch escort as William entered, cheered by the same crowds who greeted his predecessor two days before.[[125]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJardine200819-134) William occupied London and now effectively controlled the English government and the country's army, navy, and finances.[[126]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEIsrael1995852-135)[[127]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETrevor202085-136) On 22 December, Berwick arrived in Rochester with blank passports allowing them to leave England, while his guards were instructed not to impede his escape.[[128]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHuygens188162-137) Although Ailesbury and others begged him to stay, James left for France on 23 December.[[121]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJardine200817-129)

## The revolutionary settlement

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Glorious_Revolution&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: The revolutionary settlement")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1f/SA_4973-Anno_1689._De_kroning_van_Willem_III_en_Maria_Stuart.jpg/250px-SA_4973-Anno_1689._De_kroning_van_Willem_III_en_Maria_Stuart.jpg)](/wiki/File:SA_4973-Anno_1689._De_kroning_van_Willem_III_en_Maria_Stuart.jpg)

The coronation of William and Mary, by [Charles Rochussen](/wiki/Charles_Rochussen "Charles Rochussen"). [William III](/wiki/William_III_of_England "William III of England") and [Mary II](/wiki/Mary_II "Mary II") reigned jointly until her death in 1694, when William became sole monarch.

James' departure enabled William to take control of the provisional government on 28 December.[[129]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarris2006319-138) Elections were held in early January for a [Convention Parliament](/wiki/Convention_Parliament_(1689) "Convention Parliament (1689)"), which assembled on 22 January. While the Whigs had a slight majority in the [Commons](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_England "House of Commons of England"), the [Lords](/wiki/House_of_Lords "House of Lords") was dominated by the Tories, but both were led by moderates. Even Stuart loyalists like Archbishop Sancroft recognised keeping James on the throne was no longer possible. Instead, they argued his daughter Mary should either be appointed regent, or sole monarch.[[129]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarris2006319-138)

The issue was debated for next two weeks, much to the annoyance of William, who needed a swift resolution. The situation in Ireland was rapidly deteriorating, while the French had over-run large parts of the Rhineland and were preparing to attack the Dutch.[[130]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarris2006325-139) At a meeting with Danby and Halifax on 3 February, he declared he would return home if the Convention did not appoint him joint monarch, while Mary stated she would only rule jointly with her husband. Faced with this ultimatum, on 6 February Parliament declared that in choosing exile, James had abdicated and thus vacated the Crown, which was therefore offered jointly to William and Mary.[[131]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMiller1978209-140)

Historian [Tim Harris](/wiki/Timothy_J._G._Harris "Timothy J. G. Harris") argues the most radical act of the 1688 Revolution was the idea of a "contract" between ruler and people, rebutting the Stuart ideology of divine right.[[132]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarris2006329-141) While this was a victory for the Whigs, other pieces of legislation were proposed by the Tories, often with moderate Whig support, designed to protect the Anglican establishment from being undermined by future monarchs, including the [Calvinist](/wiki/Calvinist "Calvinist") William. The [Declaration of Right](/wiki/Declaration_of_Right "Declaration of Right") was a tactical compromise, setting out where James had failed, and establishing the rights of English citizens, without agreeing their cause or offering solutions. In December 1689, this was incorporated into the [Bill of Rights](/wiki/Bill_of_Rights_1689 "Bill of Rights 1689").[[133]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPincus2009292–293-142)

However, there were two areas that arguably broke new constitutional ground, both responses to what were viewed as specific abuses by James. First, the Declaration of Right made keeping a standing army without parliamentary consent illegal, overturning the [1661](/wiki/The_King%27s_Sole_Right_over_the_Militia_Act_1661 "The King's Sole Right over the Militia Act 1661") and [1662 Militia Acts](/wiki/City_of_London_Militia_Act_1662 "City of London Militia Act 1662") and vesting control of the military in Parliament, not the Crown.[[134]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarris2006341-143) The second was the [Coronation Oath Act 1688](/wiki/Coronation_Oath_Act_1688 "Coronation Oath Act 1688"); the result of James's perceived failure to comply with that taken in 1685, it established obligations owed by the monarchy to the people.[[135]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMaerGay20084-144)

At their coronation on 11 April, William and Mary swore to "govern the people of this kingdom of England, and the dominions thereunto belonging, according to the statutes in Parliament agreed on, and the laws and customs of the same". They were also to maintain the Protestant Reformed faith and "preserve inviolable the settlement of the Church of England, and its doctrine, worship, discipline and government as by law established".[[135]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMaerGay20084-144)

### Scotland

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Glorious_Revolution&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Scotland")]

Main article: [Glorious Revolution in Scotland](/wiki/Glorious_Revolution_in_Scotland "Glorious Revolution in Scotland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ab/St._Giles_and_Parliament_House_c.1647.JPG/250px-St._Giles_and_Parliament_House_c.1647.JPG)](/wiki/File:St._Giles_and_Parliament_House_c.1647.JPG)

[Parliament House, Edinburgh](/wiki/Parliament_House,_Edinburgh "Parliament House, Edinburgh"), where the [Convention of Estates](/wiki/Convention_of_Estates_(1689) "Convention of Estates (1689)") met in March 1689

While Scotland was not involved in the landing, by November 1688 only a tiny minority supported James. Many of those who accompanied William were Scots exiles, including [the Earl of Melville](/wiki/George_Melville,_1st_Earl_of_Melville "George Melville, 1st Earl of Melville"), [the Duke of Argyll](/wiki/Archibald_Campbell,_1st_Duke_of_Argyll "Archibald Campbell, 1st Duke of Argyll"), his personal chaplain [William Carstares](/wiki/William_Carstares "William Carstares") and [Gilbert Burnet](/wiki/Gilbert_Burnet "Gilbert Burnet").[[136]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarris2006165-145) News of James's flight led to celebrations and anti-Catholic riots in Edinburgh and Glasgow. Most members of the [Scottish Privy Council](/wiki/Scottish_Privy_Council "Scottish Privy Council") went to London. On 7 January 1689, they asked William to take over government. Elections were held in March for a [Scottish Convention](/wiki/Convention_of_the_Estates_of_Scotland "Convention of the Estates of Scotland"), which was also a contest between Presbyterians and Episcopalians for control of the Kirk. While only 50 of the 125 delegates were classed as Episcopalian, they were hopeful of victory since William supported the retention of bishops.[[137]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarris2006379–381-146)

On 16 March a Letter from James was read out to the convention, demanding obedience and threatening punishment for non-compliance. Public anger at its tone meant some Episcopalians stopped attending the convention, claiming to fear for their safety and others changed sides.[[138]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESzechi199430–31-147) The 1689–1691 Jacobite Rising forced William to make concessions to the Presbyterians, ended Episcopacy in Scotland and excluded a significant portion of the political class. Many later returned to the Kirk but [Non-Juring Episcopalianism](/wiki/Nonjuring_schism "Nonjuring schism") was the key determinant of [Jacobite](/wiki/Jacobitism "Jacobitism") support in [1715](/wiki/Jacobite_rising_of_1715 "Jacobite rising of 1715") and [1745](/wiki/Jacobite_rising_of_1745 "Jacobite rising of 1745").[[139]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESzechiSankey200197-148)

The English Parliament held that James 'abandoned' his throne. The Convention argued that he 'forfeited' it by his actions, as listed in the Articles of Grievances.[[140]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTE''University_of_St._Andrews''-149) On 11 April, the Convention ended James's reign and adopted the Articles of Grievances and the [Claim of Right Act](/wiki/Claim_of_Right_Act_1689 "Claim of Right Act 1689"), making Parliament the primary legislative power in Scotland.[[141]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECoward1980460-150) On 11 May, William and Mary accepted the Crown of Scotland; after their acceptance, the *Claim* and the *Articles* were read aloud, leading to an immediate debate over whether or not an endorsement of these documents was implicit in that acceptance.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*][[142]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETroost2005-151)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]

### Ireland

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Glorious_Revolution&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Ireland")]

Main article: [Williamite War in Ireland](/wiki/Williamite_War_in_Ireland "Williamite War in Ireland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c8/Andrew_Carrick_Gow_%281848-1920%29_-_A_Lost_Cause%2C_Flight_of_King_James_II_after_the_Battle_of_the_Boyne_-_N01530_-_National_Gallery.jpg/250px-Andrew_Carrick_Gow_%281848-1920%29_-_A_Lost_Cause%2C_Flight_of_King_James_II_after_the_Battle_of_the_Boyne_-_N01530_-_National_Gallery.jpg)](/wiki/File:Andrew_Carrick_Gow_(1848-1920)_-_A_Lost_Cause,_Flight_of_King_James_II_after_the_Battle_of_the_Boyne_-_N01530_-_National_Gallery.jpg)

*[A Lost Cause](/wiki/A_Lost_Cause "A Lost Cause")* by [Andrew Carrick Gow](/wiki/Andrew_Carrick_Gow "Andrew Carrick Gow"), 1888. James II departing for France from [Kinsale](/wiki/Kinsale "Kinsale") following the [Battle of the Boyne](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Boyne "Battle of the Boyne") in 1690.

After his flight from England in December 1688, James II had been given refuge by Louis XIV, who provided him financial and diplomatic assistance. Accompanied by 6,000 French troops, on 12 March 1689 he landed in Ireland, where he was backed by the majority Catholic population.[[143]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarris2006440Magennis19986–111-152) His supporters were known as "[Jacobites](/wiki/Jacobitism "Jacobitism")", and the [war in Ireland](/wiki/Williamite_War_in_Ireland "Williamite War in Ireland") was accompanied by a [rising in Scotland](/wiki/Jacobite_rising_of_1689 "Jacobite rising of 1689"); for James, the main objective was to retake England and thus he viewed both Scotland and Ireland as strategic dead ends.[[144]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcKayScott1984138–140-153) On the other hand, Louis saw them as an opportunity to divert British resources from the Low Countries, a difference in aims that was never adequately resolved.[[145]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELynn1999203-154)

James' Catholic deputy, the [Earl of Tyrconnell](/wiki/Richard_Talbot,_1st_Earl_of_Tyrconnell "Richard Talbot, 1st Earl of Tyrconnell"), had raised an [Army](/wiki/Irish_Army_(1542-1801) "Irish Army (1542-1801)") of around 36,000, although many were poorly equipped and it was almost impossible to feed, pay and supply so many.[[146]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarris2006405-155) Although they quickly occupied much of Ireland, including largely Protestant [Ulster](/wiki/Ulster "Ulster"), they were unable to capture the key northern port of [Derry](/wiki/Siege_of_Derry "Siege of Derry") and were forced to retreat at the end of July. In August, Williamite general [Schomberg](/wiki/Frederick_Schomberg,_1st_Duke_of_Schomberg "Frederick Schomberg, 1st Duke of Schomberg") landed in [Belfast Lough](/wiki/Belfast_Lough "Belfast Lough") with 15,000 reinforcements, but logistics failures meant his army stalled at [Dundalk](/wiki/Dundalk "Dundalk") and suffered heavily from sickness and desertion.[[147]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKinross199827–28-156)

The Scottish Jacobites suffered heavy losses in securing victory at [Killiecrankie](/wiki/Battle_of_Killiecrankie "Battle of Killiecrankie") in July 1689, including their leader [Viscount Dundee](/wiki/John_Graham,_1st_Viscount_Dundee "John Graham, 1st Viscount Dundee"). By May 1690 the rising had been largely suppressed, although pockets of resistance continued in the [Highlands](/wiki/Scottish_Highlands "Scottish Highlands") until early 1692. At the same time, William III assumed command of government troops in Ireland and gained an important success at the [Battle of the Boyne](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Boyne "Battle of the Boyne") in July 1690, before their naval victory at [Beachy Head](/wiki/Battle_of_Beachy_Head_(1690) "Battle of Beachy Head (1690)") gave the French temporary control of the [English Channel](/wiki/English_Channel "English Channel"). James returned to France to urge an immediate invasion of England, but the Anglo-Dutch fleet soon regained maritime supremacy, and no invasion was launched.[[148]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELynn1999215-157)

By the end of 1690, French and Jacobite troops were confined to the south and west of Ireland. Although repulsed with heavy losses at [Limerick](/wiki/Siege_of_Limerick_(1690) "Siege of Limerick (1690)") in September, William transferred command to [Godert de Ginkel](/wiki/Godert_de_Ginkel,_1st_Earl_of_Athlone "Godert de Ginkel, 1st Earl of Athlone") and returned to Flanders. Despite receiving reinforcements and a new general in the [Marquis de St Ruth](/wiki/Marquis_de_St_Ruth "Marquis de St Ruth"), the Franco-Irish army was defeated at [Aughrim](/wiki/Battle_of_Aughrim "Battle of Aughrim") on 12 July 1691; the war in Ireland ended with the [Treaty of Limerick](/wiki/Treaty_of_Limerick "Treaty of Limerick") in October, allowing the bulk of the Williamite forces to be shipped to the Low Countries.[[149]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKinross199898-158)

## Anglo-Dutch alliance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Glorious_Revolution&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Anglo-Dutch alliance")]

See also: [Nine Years' War](/wiki/Nine_Years%27_War "Nine Years' War") and [War of the Spanish Succession](/wiki/War_of_the_Spanish_Succession "War of the Spanish Succession")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/52/William_III_at_the_Battle_of_Landen.jpg/330px-William_III_at_the_Battle_of_Landen.jpg)](/wiki/File:William_III_at_the_Battle_of_Landen.jpg)

William III at the [Battle of Landen](/wiki/Battle_of_Landen "Battle of Landen") in 1693, by [Ernest Crofts](/wiki/Ernest_Crofts "Ernest Crofts")

Though he had carefully avoided making it public, William's main motive in organising the expedition had been the opportunity to bring England into [an alliance](/wiki/Grand_Alliance_(League_of_Augsburg) "Grand Alliance (League of Augsburg)") against France.[[150]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEIsrael198937-38-159) On 9 December 1688 he had already asked the States General to send a delegation of three to negotiate the conditions. On 18 February (Julian calendar) he asked the convention to support the Republic in its war against France. It refused, only consenting to pay £600,000 for the continued presence of the 17,000 Dutch troops in England.[[151]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETroost2001236-237-160) On 9 March (Gregorian calendar) the States General responded to Louis's earlier [declaration of war](/wiki/Nine_Years%27_War "Nine Years' War") by declaring war on France in return.

Before British forces could effectively take part in the war, the English army had to be reorganised. James' commander-in-chief [Louis de Duras, Earl of Feversham](/wiki/Louis_de_Duras,_2nd_Earl_of_Feversham "Louis de Duras, 2nd Earl of Feversham"), had disbanded the English army in December 1688 so it had to be effectively rebuilt from scratch. Many officers were unwilling to continue under William, while William was reluctant to trust those who had not already served under him. In addition, according to historian Jonathan Scott: "The state and discipline of the rank and file was 'deplorable'. There was a dire lack of experience and competence at every level."[[152]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEScott2000479-161) For the purpose of reforming the English army on the Dutch model William appointed Dutch officers to key positions.[[153]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVan_Nimwegen2020190-162)

The English elite also failed to secure the most important governmental posts, while English Secretaries of State primarily served as executors rather than architects of foreign policy. The development of foreign policy was largely directed by William III and [Anthonie Heinsius](/wiki/Anthonie_Heinsius "Anthonie Heinsius"), who assumed the role of Grand Pensionary of Holland following Fagel's death in late 1688. This situation caused considerable resentment in England.[[154]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETroost2005216-163)

On 19 April (Julian calendar) the Dutch delegation signed a naval treaty with England. It stipulated that the combined Anglo-Dutch fleet would always be commanded by an Englishman, even when of lower rank.[[155]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETroost2001236-164) The Dutch agreed to this to make their dominance over the English army more acceptable for the British.[[153]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVan_Nimwegen2020190-162) The treaty also specified that the two parties would contribute in the ratio of five English vessels against three Dutch vessels, meaning in practice that the Dutch navy in the future would be smaller than the English, something that caused considerable unease in the Dutch Republic.[[155]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETroost2001236-164) The [Navigation Acts](/wiki/Navigation_Acts "Navigation Acts") were not repealed.[[155]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETroost2001236-164) On 18 May, the new Parliament allowed William to declare war on France. On 9 September 1689, (Gregorian calendar), William as King of England joined the [League of Augsburg](/wiki/League_of_Augsburg "League of Augsburg") against France.[[156]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETroost2001238-165)

### The decline of the Dutch Republic

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Glorious_Revolution&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: The decline of the Dutch Republic")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dc/Battle_of_Vigo_bay_october_23_1702.jpg/250px-Battle_of_Vigo_bay_october_23_1702.jpg)](/wiki/File:Battle_of_Vigo_bay_october_23_1702.jpg)

Dutch and English ships at the [Battle of Vigo Bay](/wiki/Battle_of_Vigo_Bay "Battle of Vigo Bay"), 1702

Having England as an ally meant that the military situation of the Republic was strongly improved, which allowed William to be uncompromising in his position towards France. The Dutch successfully secured and expanded their positions in the Spanish Netherlands, while halting French territorial expansion,[[157]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVan_Nimwegen2020354-166) but these military campaigns were very expensive. In 1712, at the end of the [War of the Spanish Succession](/wiki/War_of_the_Spanish_Succession "War of the Spanish Succession"), the Republic was financially exhausted and was forced to let its fleet deteriorate, which played a role in Britain becoming the preeminent global maritime power.[[158]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVriesWoude1997673–687-167)

The Dutch economy, already burdened by the high national debt and concomitant high taxation, suffered from the other European states' [protectionist](/wiki/Protectionist "Protectionist") policies, which its weakened fleet was no longer able to resist. To make matters worse, the main Dutch trading and banking houses moved much of their activity from Amsterdam to London after 1688. Between 1688 and 1720, world trade dominance shifted from the Republic to Great Britain.[[158]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVriesWoude1997673–687-167)

## Assessment and historiography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Glorious_Revolution&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Assessment and historiography")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/09/Joseph_Mallord_William_Turner_%281775-1851%29_-_The_Prince_of_Orange%2C_William_III%2C_Embarked_from_Holland%2C_and_Landed_at_Torbay%2C_November_4th%2C_1688%2C_after_a_Stormy_Passage_-_N00369_-_National_Gallery.jpg/250px-thumbnail.jpg)](/wiki/File:Joseph_Mallord_William_Turner_(1775-1851)_-_The_Prince_of_Orange,_William_III,_Embarked_from_Holland,_and_Landed_at_Torbay,_November_4th,_1688,_after_a_Stormy_Passage_-_N00369_-_National_Gallery.jpg)

*[The Prince of Orange Landing at Torbay](/wiki/The_Prince_of_Orange,_William_III,_Embarked_from_Holland,_and_Landed_at_Torbay,_November_4th,_1688,_after_a_Stormy_Passage "The Prince of Orange, William III, Embarked from Holland, and Landed at Torbay, November 4th, 1688, after a Stormy Passage")* by [J. M. W. Turner](/wiki/J._M._W._Turner "J. M. W. Turner"), 1832

While the 1688 revolution was labeled "Glorious" by Protestant preachers two decades later,[[159]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHertzler1987-168) its historiography is complex, and its assessment disputed. [Thomas Macaulay](/wiki/Thomas_Macaulay "Thomas Macaulay")'s account of the Revolution in *[The History of England from the Accession of James the Second](/wiki/The_History_of_England_from_the_Accession_of_James_the_Second "The History of England from the Accession of James the Second")* exemplifies the "[Whig history](/wiki/Whig_history "Whig history")" narrative of the Revolution as a largely consensual and bloodless triumph of English common sense, confirming and strengthening its institutions of tempered popular liberty and limited monarchy.[[160]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPincus20095-169) [Edmund Burke](/wiki/Edmund_Burke "Edmund Burke") set the tone for that interpretation when he proclaimed: "The Revolution was made to preserve our ancient indisputable laws and liberties, and that ancient constitution of government which is our only security for law and liberty."[[161]](#cite_note-170)

An alternative narrative emphasizes William's successful foreign invasion from the Netherlands, and the size of the corresponding military operation. Several researchers have emphasized that aspect, particularly after the third centenary of the event in 1988.[[162]](#cite_note-171) The historian [J. R. Jones](/wiki/J._R._Jones "J. R. Jones") suggested that the invasion "should be seen ... as the first and arguably the only decisive phase of the Nine Years' War."[[163]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETaylor1994466-172) [John Childs](/wiki/John_Childs_(historian) "John Childs (historian)") added that "there was no natural political turmoil in England in 1688", or "at least not of sufficient consequence to produce the overthrow of a king."[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChilds1988418-52) [Jonathan Israel](/wiki/Jonathan_Israel "Jonathan Israel") also stresses the importance of the Dutch aspect by arguing that, due the Dutch occupation of London, parliament was hardly free when they decided to accept William as their king.[[164]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEIsrael2003130-173)

It has been argued that the invasion aspect had been downplayed as a result of British pride and effective Dutch propaganda, trying to depict the course of events as a largely internal English affair.[[165]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJardine200827-174) As the invitation was initiated by figures who had little influence, the legacy of the Glorious Revolution has been described as a successful propaganda act by William to cover up and justify his invasion.[[166]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchwoerer1977-175)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] The claim that William was fighting for the Protestant cause in England was used to great effect to disguise the military, cultural and political impact that the Dutch regime had on England.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/08/Church_Pennant.svg/250px-Church_Pennant.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Church_Pennant.svg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/42/William_of_Orange_Expeditionary_Banner_1688.svg/250px-William_of_Orange_Expeditionary_Banner_1688.svg.png)](/wiki/File:William_of_Orange_Expeditionary_Banner_1688.svg)

Expeditionary Banner used by William of Orange as Commander-in-Chief[[167]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERobb1962267-176)

A third version, proposed by [Steven Pincus](/wiki/Steven_Pincus "Steven Pincus"), underplays the invasion aspect but unlike the Whig narrative views the Revolution as a divisive and violent event that involved all classes of the English population, not just the main aristocratic protagonists.[[168]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPincus2009-177)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] Pincus argues that his interpretation echoes the widely held view of the Revolution in its aftermath, starting with its revolutionary labelling. Pincus argues that it was momentous especially when looking at the alternative that James was trying to enact – a powerful centralised autocratic state, using French-style "state-building". England's role in Europe and the country's political economy in the 17th century rebuts the view of many late-20th-century historians that nothing revolutionary occurred during the Glorious Revolution of 1688–89. Pincus says it was not a placid turn of events.[[169]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPincus2009?-178)

In diplomacy and economics William III transformed the English state's ideology and policies. This occurred not because William III was an outsider who inflicted foreign notions on England but because foreign affairs and political economy were at the core of the English revolutionaries' agenda. The revolution of 1688–89 cannot be fathomed in isolation. It would have been inconceivable without the changes resulting from the events of the 1640s and 1650s. The ideas accompanying the Glorious Revolution were rooted in the mid-century upheavals. The 17th century was a century of revolution in England, deserving of the same scholarly attention that 'modern' revolutions attract.[[169]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPincus2009?-178)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]

James II tried building a powerful militarised state on the [mercantilist](/wiki/Mercantilist "Mercantilist") assumption that the world's wealth was necessarily finite, and empires were created by taking land from other states. The [East India Company](/wiki/East_India_Company "East India Company") was thus an ideal tool to create a vast new English imperial dominion by warring with the Dutch and the [Mughal Empire](/wiki/Mughal_Empire "Mughal Empire") in India. After 1689 came an alternative understanding of economics, which saw Britain as a commercial rather than an agrarian society. It led to the foundation of the [Bank of England](/wiki/Bank_of_England "Bank of England"), the creation of Europe's first widely circulating credit currency and the commencement of the "[Age of Projectors](/wiki/Projector_(business) "Projector (business)")".[[170]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWennerlind2011109-179) This subsequently gave weight to the view, advocated most famously by [Adam Smith](/wiki/Adam_Smith "Adam Smith") in 1776, that wealth was created by human endeavour and was thus potentially infinite.[[171]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPincus2009369–370-180)

[Karl Marx](/wiki/Karl_Marx "Karl Marx") viewed the revolution as essentially conservative in nature, writing that it was shaped by an alliance between English commercial and industrial [bourgeoisie](/wiki/Bourgeoisie "Bourgeoisie") and increasingly commercialized large land owners.[[172]](#cite_note-181)[[173]](#cite_note-182)

### Impact

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Glorious_Revolution&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Impact")]

As a coup, albeit largely bloodless, its legitimacy rests in the will expressed separately by the Scottish and English Parliaments according to their respective legal processes.[[174]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELynch1992302-183) On this point, the [Earl of Shaftesbury](/wiki/Anthony_Ashley-Cooper,_3rd_Earl_of_Shaftesbury "Anthony Ashley-Cooper, 3rd Earl of Shaftesbury") declared in 1689, "The Parliament of England is that supreme and absolute power, which gives life and motion to the English government".[[175]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBradley200728-184) The Revolution established the primacy of [parliamentary sovereignty](/wiki/Parliamentary_sovereignty "Parliamentary sovereignty"), a principle still relevant in consultation with the 15 [Commonwealth realms](/wiki/Commonwealth_realm "Commonwealth realm") regarding [succession](/wiki/Succession_to_the_British_throne "Succession to the British throne") issues.[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] The [Bill of Rights 1689](/wiki/Bill_of_Rights_1689 "Bill of Rights 1689") formally established a system of [constitutional monarchy](/wiki/Constitutional_monarchy "Constitutional monarchy") and ended moves towards [absolute monarchy](/wiki/Absolute_monarchy "Absolute monarchy") by restricting the power of the monarch, who could no longer suspend laws, levy taxes, make royal appointments or maintain a standing army during peacetime without Parliament's consent. The [British Army](/wiki/British_Army "British Army") remains the military arm of Parliament, not the monarch, although the Crown is the source of all military executive authority.[[176]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWindeyer1938-185)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]

Unlike the 1639 to 1653 [Wars of the Three Kingdoms](/wiki/Wars_of_the_Three_Kingdoms "Wars of the Three Kingdoms"), most ordinary people in England and Scotland were relatively untouched by the "Glorious Revolution", the majority of the bloodshed taking place in Ireland. As a consequence, some historians suggest that in England at least it more closely resembles a coup d'état, rather than a social upheaval such as the [French Revolution](/wiki/French_Revolution "French Revolution").[[177]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWebb1995166-186)[[j]](#cite_note-188) This view is consistent with the original meaning of "revolution" as a circular process under which an old system of values is restored to its original position, with England's supposed "ancient constitution" being reasserted, rather than formed anew.[[179]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMitchell2009xvi,_xviii,_xix-189) Contemporary English political thought, as expressed in [John Locke](/wiki/John_Locke "John Locke")'s then popular [social contract](/wiki/Social_contract "Social contract") theory,[[180]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMasonSmith2004-190) linked to [George Buchanan](/wiki/George_Buchanan "George Buchanan")'s view of the contractual agreement between the monarch and their subjects,[[181]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTE''De_Jure_Regni_apud_Scotos''2015-191) an argument used by the Scottish Parliament as justification for the Claim of Right.

Under the [Coronation Oath Act 1688](/wiki/Coronation_Oath_Act_1688 "Coronation Oath Act 1688"), William had sworn to maintain the primacy of the Church of England, which both his native [Dutch Reformed Church](/wiki/Dutch_Reformed_Church "Dutch Reformed Church") and the Church of Scotland viewed as ideologically suspect in both doctrine and use of bishops. This required a certain degree of religious flexibility on his part, especially as he needed to placate his Catholic allies, Spain and Emperor Leopold.[[182]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEIsrael2003137–138-192) Despite promising legal toleration for Catholics in his *Declaration* of October 1688, William failed due to domestic opposition.[[183]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEIsrael200320-193) The [Act of Toleration 1689](/wiki/Act_of_Toleration_1689 "Act of Toleration 1689") granted relief to Nonconformists but [Catholic emancipation](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Relief_Act_1829 "Roman Catholic Relief Act 1829") would be delayed until 1829.[[184]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHolmes20073-194)

News of the Glorious Revolution reached the English colonies in North America in 1689, leading to [a revolt in Boston](/wiki/1689_Boston_revolt "1689 Boston revolt") and the dissolution of the [Dominion of New England](/wiki/Dominion_of_New_England "Dominion of New England").[[185]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarnes1960234–257-195) Notably, the Glorious Revolution's principles influenced later human rights frameworks, including the [United Nations Universal Declaration of Human Rights](/wiki/United_Nations_Universal_Declaration_of_Human_Rights "United Nations Universal Declaration of Human Rights") and the [European Convention on Human Rights](/wiki/European_Convention_on_Human_Rights "European Convention on Human Rights").[[186]](#cite_note-196)

6. **[^](#cite_ref-105)** There were seventy-five vessels of the confederated Dutch navy. Forty-nine were warships of more than twenty cannon. Transports included seventy-six [fluyts](/wiki/Fluyt "Fluyt") for the soldiers, one hundred and twenty small transports with five thousand horses, and about seventy supply vessels.
